quote: I just wanna see what the options are now, compared to what they were then. Because I know that there were credits that were available then that I don't know if they're available now.
stewart_read: Zachary hands Meg a rich interest_in_solar answer: bill pain, a prior pitch he regrets not acting on, and a specific concern about tax credits. The schema coaching note on this item is explicit — 'Top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Bottom setters check the box and move on.' Meg spent roughly 15 seconds on it before pivoting to the bill question. The regret signal ('I just kinda regret not doing it then') was the emotional anchor of the entire call and Meg walked past it entirely.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg may have been deliberately keeping pace to avoid losing a cooperative customer to a long call. The 'I'll be quick' opener at 00:15 suggests she was managing call length intentionally.
coaching_implication: Pull this moment in the 1-on-1. Ask Meg: 'What did you hear Zachary say here?' The regret signal is the highest-leverage emotional anchor on this call — it's the reason he filled out the form again after two years. A single reflective question ('What made you decide to try again now?') would have deepened the emotional investment before the bill question. Schema verify.interest_in_solar depth_grading is the governing standard.

quote: Yeah. So the the big federal tax credits, those did end up going away.
stewart_read: Two schema drift issues in the same 90-second window. First, Meg states the federal tax credits 'did end up going away' — the schema notes the credit may still be claimable for year-end installs and explicitly states Ion can no longer guarantee it but it remains possible. A flat 'they went away' is inaccurate and forecloses a potential value point. Second, at 03:01 Meg states the credit threshold as 650, not the schema-specified 670. Neither error is catastrophic on this call, but both are factual gaps that could create closer-handoff friction if Zachary raises either point again.
coaching_implication: Two separate script adherence gaps to address: (1) Tax credit language — Meg should use the schema-approved framing: 'We can no longer guarantee the tax credit, but it may still be claimable for installs completed before year-end.' (2) Credit threshold — confirm with Kenny whether 650 or 670 is the active number and align the floor. Do not let Meg carry the wrong number into future calls.

quote: Mine is. I don't want it run until I'm ready.
stewart_read: Zachary's credit-pull boundary is a trust signal, not an objection. He's telling Meg — and by extension the closer — that he wants to control the pace of commitment. Meg handled it correctly in the moment ('Yeah. I don't even have the pallet too') and moved on without friction. The coaching value here is in the handoff: the closer must know not to rush the credit pull or frame it as a routine step. Zachary will read that as a boundary violation.
coaching_implication: No rep correction needed here — Meg handled it cleanly. Flag for the handoff brief: closer should frame the credit pull as customer-initiated and customer-controlled, not as a procedural step Ion runs automatically.

quote: I am seeing a tree in the front yard that's probably gonna put some shade on the roof. Is that still there?
stewart_read: Meg is using Google Maps proactively before the call to assess site conditions — this is a credibility-building move that signals preparation and professionalism. It also surfaces a potential shading concern before the customer raises it. The follow-up at 00:56 ('if my design team looks at it and they say, hey. We might need to remove the tree to get more sun exposure. Is that something you're open to?') is a clean qualification question. Zachary's answer ('It is my tree, so yes') is an enthusiasm signal — he's already mentally willing to modify his property for solar. Meg acknowledged it but did not anchor on it.
coaching_implication: Surface the Google Maps prep as a positive behavior worth reinforcing — it's a legitimacy-builder that most setters skip. Separately, Zachary's tree-removal openness is a mild enthusiasm signal that could have been reflected back: 'That tells me you're serious about making this work — that's exactly the kind of flexibility that gets a system designed right.' One sentence of reflection would have deepened his investment.

quote: Well, I mean, will you be able to send me some program information in the meantime?
stewart_read: Zachary is not asking for a proposal or design — he's asking for general program information. Meg correctly holds the line on not sending a design ('we don't even build a design without having anything scheduled beforehand'), but then offers to send 'basics of the program' via email. This is not a violation of the send_proposal_before_meeting hard rule, which governs proposals and designs specifically. However, the email offer creates a cooling-off window: Zachary now has a reason to treat Monday as optional — he can read the email and decide whether to show up. The coaching concern is the appointment commitment leak, not a protocol violation.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg may have calculated that a small concession (program basics email) was worth making to secure the tentative booking. If Zachary was about to disengage entirely, the email offer may have been the price of keeping him on the calendar.
coaching_implication: The email offer itself is not a protocol violation, but it creates a no-show risk by giving Zachary an information path that doesn't require the appointment. In the 1-on-1, ask Meg: 'What happens if Zachary reads the email and decides he has enough to make a decision without the Monday call?' The stronger move would have been to redirect the information request into the appointment itself: 'Everything I'd send you, the specialist walks through live — that's actually the point of Monday's call. Let's lock that in first.'

quote: My usage is gone way up, wasn't it?
stewart_read: Zachary surfaces his own bill pain unprompted — usage up roughly 50%, from ~1,500 to ~2,300 kWh. Meg responds with a weather explanation ('this last winter being so freaking cold') and then a neutral acknowledgment. This is the second bill-anchor moment on the call and the stronger one — Zachary is doing the emotional work himself. Meg captured the data but did not flip it. The schema is explicit: 'capture alone is not enough — the bill must be FLIPPED into emotional alignment-against-the-utility.' The flip was available and unused.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg may have judged that Zachary was already engaged enough that a flip would feel like piling on. The call was already moving toward a booking.
coaching_implication: This is the clearest flip opportunity on the call. Zachary handed Meg the number and the emotion. A suggested redirect (not verbatim): acknowledge the usage spike, then turn it toward the utility — 'And that's exactly what they're counting on. Every cold winter, every hot summer, your bill goes up and there's nothing you can do about it. That's the problem solar solves.' No fabricated numbers needed — Zachary's own 1,500-to-2,300 kWh jump is the leverage. Schema coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion and bill_anchoring both apply.

quote: Let's tentatively plan for Monday, you said, at 03:30?
stewart_read: Zachary sets the 'tentative' framing himself, and Meg accepts it without converting to a firm commitment. The button-up protocol requires: appointment datetime confirmed, one-hour block stated, phone appointment specified, no-driving/at-computer anchor, 'is that fair' handshake, and text follow-up promise. None of these elements appear in the close. Meg's follow-up at 08:23 ('if I don't hear back from you, we'll call Monday morning') actually reinforces the tentative framing by building in an escape hatch — Zachary can simply not respond and the appointment dissolves.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg may have read Zachary as cooperative enough that a full button-up would feel over-engineered. The call had been smooth and she may have judged that pressing harder would create friction.
coaching_implication: Walk Meg through the button-up checklist against this call — she missed every element. The 'is that fair' handshake is the single highest-leverage miss: it converts a tentative into a verbal commitment. The no-driving/computer anchor is the second — without it, no-show risk climbs. The Monday morning confirmation call Meg promised is a partial recovery, but it's a weaker substitute. Given the comp model (paid on sits, not bookings), a fragile tentative appointment is a paycheck risk, not just a coaching gap.
